cross_model.reasoning: Step 1 — Query/constraints: user wants a wedding-guest appropriate dress, preferably burgundy, nothing too tight (loose fit). Step 2 — Set A (CLIP): 4/5 items are dresses but colors are mostly biscuit, dark mink (brown), bright red and black; one item is a wine-colored top (not a dress). Only the red nylon midi dress (#3) and possibly the trench-coat midi (#1) offer looser silhouettes; several items (velvet strappy, top) are either too tight or not dresses. Overall color match to burgundy is weak. Step 3 — Set B (FashionCLIP): 3/5 items are dresses including an aubergine short balloon dress (good burgundy-like color + looser shape) and draped velvet/dark-brown and lace midi/long options that read formal and elegant; two items are non-dress (skirt, leather top). Set B contains clearer burgundy/garnet/aubergine matches and more elegant fabrics (lace, velvet) suitable for a wedding. Step 4 — Hard constraints: FashionCLIP better satisfies color (aubergine/garnet) and occasion; CLIP has more items that miss the burgundy color or are non-dresses. Both sets include some non-dress or tight items but CLIP has more mismatch. Step 5 — Occasion appropriateness: FashionCLIP’s lace and velvet pieces read more formal/elegant for a wedding; CLIP’s selection includes casual colors and a strappy black velvet dress (tight) and a red casual tiered dress that may be less formal. Step 6 — Visual diversity: Both sets offer a range (short, midi, velvet, lace), but FashionCLIP provides more ceremony-appropriate textures and a stronger burgundy palette; CLIP’s diversity is weaker in color relevance. Step 7 — Overall intent match: FashionCLIP more closely reflects the user intent (burgundy tones, elegant fabrics, at least one loose silhouette). Step 8 — Scores: CLIP = 3 (average — some dresses but weak color/occasion fit, some non-dress items); FashionCLIP = 4 (above average — better color match and more elegant/wedding-appropriate options, minor irrelevant items). Step 9 — Winner: fashion_clip.

cross_model.reasoning:
Step 1: Query asks for an elegant green skirt for a wedding (category: skirts, color: green, occasion: wedding_guest).
Step 2: Set A (CLIP) — contains 5 skirts: two clear greens (100% wool dark bottle green midi; knit midi in moss green) that match color and are potentially elegant; three others are neutral/beige/tobacco and less suitable for a green-focused search. Visuals show simple pencil and knit shapes plus flared/beige options; overall useful but only two clearly green, and material/formality varies (knit may be casual).
Step 3: Set B (FashionCLIP) — contains the same dark bottle green wool midi and the moss green knit, plus a dark green limited-edition minimal suede leather skirt (high quality, more formal/elegant and very suitable for a wedding), plus tobacco and beige options. Visuals include the suede dark green piece which reads more polished and occasion-appropriate.
Step 4: Relevance to hard constraints — both sets include two green skirts and all items are skirts; FashionCLIP adds a clearly higher-end dark green suede option that better satisfies the "elegant" and wedding requirement. Price range not constrained, but FashionCLIP offers a premium choice.
Step 5: Occasion appropriateness — CLIP has acceptable green options but the knit may be too casual; FashionCLIP’s suede leather skirt provides a more formal/elegant option suited to a wedding.
Step 6: Visual diversity — CLIP offers a mix of silhouettes but fewer elevated/elegant green choices; FashionCLIP provides more meaningful variety in material and formality (including suede leather) increasing usefulness for a wedding look.
Step 7: Overall intent — user wants an elegant green skirt for a wedding. FashionCLIP better reflects that intent by including a formal-looking dark green suede skirt alongside the wool option; CLIP is adequate but less targeted to "elegant".

cross_model.reasoning:
Step 1: Query asks for a party/night-out red top that stands out. Hard constraints: category = tops, color = red, occasion = party.

Visuals show mostly basic knits and one wine bustier-style top; overall styles are casual/weekday knits and one dress. Some items (yellow jumper, dress) are outside the requested top/color constraints or are less party-focused. Variety is limited toward plain knit silhouettes.
Step 3 (Set B - FashionCLIP): Items include a red slogan T‑shirt (casual), a garnet 100% leather mini top (very party-appropriate and standout), the same plain knit shoulder‑pad top (overlap), a wine polyamide corset-like top (dressy), and a deep‑neck red jumper. Visuals show more evening/statement options (leather, corset) plus casual and knit choices.
Step 4 (Relevance to constraints): Both sets contain red tops, but CLIP includes a yellow jumper and a dress; FashionCLIP stays much closer to tops and red/garnet/wine tones and includes more explicit party pieces (leather/corset). FashionCLIP better matches category+occasion+“stands out”.
Step 5 (Occasion appropriateness): CLIP’s items lean casual/office or basic knit; FashionCLIP contains clear night-out pieces (leather mini top, corset top) that better convey party formality and showiness.
Step 6 (Visual diversity): FashionCLIP offers greater meaningful diversity (casual tee, leather mini, corset, knit, deep‑neck jumper). CLIP is more homogeneous (knits) with one dress and an off-color item.
Step 7 (Overall intent): FashionCLIP better reflects the user’s intent for a standout party top by offering dressier, statement options while still keeping red/wine tones.
Step 8 (Scores): CLIP set: 3 — average relevance (some correct red tops but several items are off-target or not party-oriented). FashionCLIP set: 4 — above average (better match to occasion, more standout options, minor redundant/casual items).
Step 9 (Winner): fashion_clip.

cross_model.reasoning:
Step 1: Query asks for a navy shirt for a smart-casual dinner. Hard constraints: category = shirts, color = navy blue, occasion = smart_casual.

Step 3 (Set B - FashionCLIP): 4 of 5 are shirts (one labelled cardigan/polo). It includes a clear navy shirt with pockets/tie and a navy fine-knit polo (navy color present twice). Remaining items include striped and grey shirts and one high‑end suede piece (less practical but still a shirt-like item). Visuals show two true navy options (a navy shirt and a navy knit) suitable for smart-casual.
Step 4 (Relevance to constraints): FashionCLIP better matches the explicit color constraint (two navy pieces) and category (mostly shirts). CLIP has fewer navy matches and includes a dress.
Step 5 (Occasion appropriateness): FashionCLIP’s navy shirt and fine-knit polo are well suited to smart-casual dinner; CLIP’s options lean more casual/oversized and include a dress that doesn’t fit the request.
Step 6 (Visual diversity): CLIP offers more stylistic variety (checks, stripes, oversized) but less alignment to navy; FashionCLIP offers useful variety while staying closer to navy and smart-casual (button shirt, knit polo, plus other shirt styles).
Step 7 (Overall intent): FashionCLIP better reflects the user intent (navy shirt for smart-casual). CLIP has some relevant items but includes mismatches (pink check, dress) and fewer navy choices.
Step 8 (scores): CLIP = 3 (average — some correct category items but only one clear navy and one irrelevant dress). FashionCLIP = 4 (above average — multiple navy options, good smart-casual fit; minor mismatches like an expensive suede piece and a mislabeled cardigan/polo).
Step 9 (winner): fashion_clip — better satisfies color and occasion with more relevant shirt choices.
